args.result:
## Audit Summary: Providers & Middleware — D:\Photography-Pixel-Hub

I read all 11 requested files plus supporting modules (`src/types/auth.ts`, `src/lib/auth/errors.ts`, `src/lib/constants/routes.ts`, `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx`, `src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx`, `src/app/error.tsx`, and confirmed absence of `src/app/global-error.tsx`). Findings below, ordered by severity.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL — Open Redirect Vulnerability
**File:** `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx:34, 60`
```ts
const redirect = searchParams.get("redirect") ?? ROUTES.DASHBOARD;  // L34
...
router.push(redirect);  // L60
```
The `redirect` query param is used directly in `router.push()` with **zero validation** that it is an internal/relative path. An attacker can craft `https://yoursite.com/login?redirect=https://evil.com` (or `//evil.com`, `/\evil.com`) and, after a victim logs in, redirect them off-site. This is a textbook open redirect, often leveraged for phishing. The middleware sets the param safely (`loginUrl.searchParams.set("redirect", pathname)` — always internal), but the login page trusts arbitrary client-supplied values.
**Fix:** Validate that `redirect` starts with `/` and does not start with `//` / `/\`, else fall back to `ROUTES.DASHBOARD`.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL — SessionProvider Never Synced With Server Session (Dead Context)
**File:** `src/providers/session-provider.tsx`
The provider initializes `user=null` / `status="unauthenticated"` and exposes `setUser`/`setStatus`, but **nothing ever populates it from the server**. I confirmed `src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx` contains no `useSession`/`setUser`/`getSession`/`getCurrentUser` calls. Consequences:
- Any component calling `useSession()` always sees an unauthenticated user on the client.
- The `status` type includes `"loading"` but it is **never used** — initial state jumps straight to `"unauthenticated"`, so there is no loading/hydration phase.
- This is an SSR/client boundary break: the server knows the session (via `getSession()`/JWT), but the client context is permanently stale.
**Fix:** Either hydrate the provider from a server component (pass the server `SessionUser` as a prop and seed initial state), or fetch session on mount and set status to `"loading"` until resolved. Better still, consider using Next.js `cookies()` server-side and dropping the client session context if not needed.

---

### 🟠 HIGH — No `global-error.tsx` (Root Layout Errors Unhandled)
Confirmed: `src/app/global-error.tsx` does **not** exist. Only `src/app/error.tsx` (and `src/app/p/[token]/error.tsx`) exist. By Next.js design, `error.tsx` does **not** catch errors thrown in the root `layout.tsx` itself. If a provider (`ThemeProvider`/`SessionProvider`/`QueryProvider`) or the `<Toaster>` throws during root layout render, the user gets the default Next.js error page, not the branded `error.tsx`. 
**Fix:** Add `src/app/global-error.tsx` (must render its own `<html>`/`<body>`).

---

### 🟠 MEDIUM — Missing Security Headers in Middleware
**File:** `middleware.ts`
Headers set: `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy`, `X-DNS-Prefetch-Control`. **Missing notable headers:**
- **`Content-Security-Policy`** (CSP) — most important omission.
- **`Strict-Transport-Security`** (HSTS) — should be set in production.
- `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy` / `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy` (optional hardening).
**Fix:** Add at least a CSP and HSTS (production-only).

---

### 🟡 MEDIUM — Two Parallel Auth Systems, Middleware Only Checks One

`PROTECTED_PREFIXES = ["/dashboard"]` and `AUTH_ROUTES = ["/login"]` only check authentication, not authorization. **Files:** `src/lib/supabase/server.ts`, `client.ts`, `admin.ts` — all use `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!`, `...ANON_KEY!`, `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!`. **File:** `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx:34, 60`

```ts
const redirect = searchParams.get("redirect") ?? ROUTES.DASHBOARD;  // L34
...
router.push(redirect);  // L60
```
The `redirect` query param is used directly in `router.push()` with **zero validation** that it is an internal/relative path. <think>Now I have a critical finding: the login page uses `searchParams.get("redirect") ?? ROUTES.DASHBOARD` and then `router.push(redirect)`. There's NO validation that `redirect` is a relative/internal path. This is an **open redirect vulnerability** — an attacker can craft a URL like `/login?redirect=https://evil.com` or `/login?redirect=//evil.com` and after successful login, the user gets redirected to the attacker's site.

This is a real security issue. Let me confirm the full login page to see the ROUTES import and whether there's any validation. I have enough context though.

Let me now consolidate my findings. I have enough evidence to write a thorough audit. Let me also quickly verify whether the middleware security headers include any missing ones (like Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security). I can see from the middleware it sets:
- X-Frame-Options: DENY
- X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
- Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
- Permissions-Policy
- X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: on

Missing notable headers: Content-Security-Policy (CSP), Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS). These are worth flagging.

Now let me also think about the SessionProvider issue. The SessionProvider uses local React state initialized to `null`/`unauthenticated`. There's NO syncing with the server session. So:
- The SessionProvider's `user` state starts as `null` (unauthenticated) on the client always
- There's no mechanism to populate it from the server session (getSession/getCurrentUser)
- This means components using `useSession()` will always see `unauthenticated` until something calls `setUser`
- This is a significant SSR/client boundary issue — the client doesn't know about the session

Also, there's a potential hydration/SSR issue: SessionProvider is a client component wrapped in the root layout (server component). That's fine. But the status starts as "unauthenticated" not "loading". On initial mount, if there's a server session, the client doesn't know. The status type includes "loading" but it's never used as initial state — starts at "unauthenticated".

Also there's a subtle bug in `handleSetUser`: it sets status based on value truthiness, but `setStatus` is exposed directly in the context value too, so a consumer could call `setStatus("loading")` without changing user. That's a minor inconsistency but not a bug per se.

Let me also note: the middleware `verifySession` only checks token validity (signature + expiry), NOT role. So all authenticated users can access all `/dashboard/*` routes at the middleware level. Role-based protection happens only in server components/actions via `requireRole`. This is acceptable but worth noting — middleware does authn, not authz.

Another middleware issue: the `redirect` param is set but never validated in middleware either. When redirecting unauthenticated users from `/dashboard/foo` to `/login?redirect=/dashboard/foo`, that's fine because pathname is internal. But the login page then trusts the redirect param without validation. The vulnerability is in the login page.

Let me also check: the matcher excludes `_next/static`, `_next/image`, `favicon.ico`, `logo`, `icons`, `images`. But it does NOT exclude API routes or other static assets. Actually that's fine — middleware runs on API routes too. But wait, the matcher runs middleware on everything except those excluded paths. The security headers get applied to all. But there might be an issue: middleware runs on API routes too, and does the auth check. For API routes that aren't protected, the auth check runs but since they don't match PROTECTED_PREFIXES or AUTH_ROUTES, they just pass through with security headers. That's fine.

However, there's a potential issue: the matcher does not exclude `/api` routes, so every API request triggers a `jwtVerify` call (which is async). This adds latency to all API routes even public ones. Minor performance note.

Actually wait — `verifySession` is called unconditionally for EVERY request, even public pages like the home page `/`. This is wasteful but not a bug. It does an async JWT verification even when not needed. Could be optimized to only verify when on protected/auth routes. Minor.

Let me also note the Supabase server client. The `createSupabaseServerClient` uses the anon key and cookie-based session. But the auth system here is JWT-based (jose), NOT Supabase Auth. There's a question of whether Supabase auth is even used. The session.ts uses a custom JWT cookie `pph-session`, while Supabase SSR manages its own cookies. These are two separate auth systems. If the app uses custom JWT auth (session.ts) for the dashboard but also has Supabase clients, there might be confusion. But the Supabase clients might be for data access only (with RLS). This is worth flagging as a potential architectural concern — two auth systems coexisting, and the middleware only checks the custom JWT, not Supabase sessions.

Let me note the `setAll` in supabase server client silently swallows errors when called from Server Components — that's documented and fine, but it means session refresh only works if middleware handles it. But the middleware here doesn't use Supabase at all (uses jose JWT). So Supabase session refresh isn't handled by middleware. This could be an issue if Supabase auth is used. But if Supabase is only for data (anon key + RLS), then it's fine.

Now, the admin client uses `process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!` with non-null assertion. If the env var is missing, this creates a client with `undefined` key which would fail at request time with a confusing error rather than a clear startup error. Minor robustness issue. Same for the other Supabase clients using `!`.

Let me also check the provider order issue. The root layout wraps:
```
ThemeProvider > SessionProvider > QueryProvider > children + Toaster
```
- ThemeProvider outermost: correct (theme should wrap everything, including for SSR class on html).
- SessionProvider inside ThemeProvider: fine.
- QueryProvider inside SessionProvider: The task asks "SessionProvider before QueryProvider if they depend on each other". QueryProvider (React Query) doesn't depend on SessionProvider here. But if queries need the session user, then SessionProvider should be outside QueryProvider so queries can access session. Currently SessionProvider IS outside QueryProvider, so that's correct ordering. Good.
- Toaster is inside QueryProvider — that's fine, Toaster doesn't need QueryClient. Could be outside but not an issue.

One issue: ThemeProvider is applied to `<body>` but the `suppressHydrationWarning` is on `<html>` — that's the correct pattern for next-themes. Good. ## Audit Summary: Providers & Middleware — D:\Photography-Pixel-Hub

I read all 11 requested files plus supporting modules (`src/types/auth.ts`, `src/lib/auth/errors.ts`, `src/lib/constants/routes.ts`, `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx`, `src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx`, `src/app/error.tsx`, and confirmed absence of `src/app/global-error.tsx`). Findings below, ordered by severity.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL — Open Redirect Vulnerability
**File:** `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx:34, 60`
```ts
const redirect = searchParams.get("redirect") ?? ROUTES.DASHBOARD;  // L34
...
router.push(redirect);  // L60
```
The `redirect` query param is used directly in `router.push()` with **zero validation** that it is an internal/relative path. An attacker can craft `https://yoursite.com/login?redirect=https://evil.com` (or `//evil.com`, `/\evil.com`) and, after a victim logs in, redirect them off-site. This is a textbook open redirect, often leveraged for phishing. The middleware sets the param safely (`loginUrl.searchParams.set("redirect", pathname)` — always internal), but the login page trusts arbitrary client-supplied values.
**Fix:** Validate that `redirect` starts with `/` and does not start with `//` / `/\`, else fall back to `ROUTES.DASHBOARD`.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL — SessionProvider Never Synced With Server Session (Dead Context)
**File:** `src/providers/session-provider.tsx`
The provider initializes `user=null` / `status="unauthenticated"` and exposes `setUser`/`setStatus`, but **nothing ever populates it from the server**. I confirmed `src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx` contains no `useSession`/`setUser`/`getSession`/`getCurrentUser` calls. Consequences:
- Any component calling `useSession()` always sees an unauthenticated user on the client.
- The `status` type includes `"loading"` but it is **never used** — initial state jumps straight to `"unauthenticated"`, so there is no loading/hydration phase.
- This is an SSR/client boundary break: the server knows the session (via `getSession()`/JWT), but the client context is permanently stale.
**Fix:** Either hydrate the provider from a server component (pass the server `SessionUser` as a prop and seed initial state), or fetch session on mount and set status to `"loading"` until resolved. Better still, consider using Next.js `cookies()` server-side and dropping the client session context if not needed.

---

### 🟠 HIGH — No `global-error.tsx` (Root Layout Errors Unhandled)
Confirmed: `src/app/global-error.tsx` does **not** exist. Only `src/app/error.tsx` (and `src/app/p/[token]/error.tsx`) exist. By Next.js design, `error.tsx` does **not** catch errors thrown in the root `layout.tsx` itself. If a provider (`ThemeProvider`/`SessionProvider`/`QueryProvider`) or the `<Toaster>` throws during root layout render, the user gets the default Next.js error page, not the branded `error.tsx`. 
**Fix:** Add `src/app/global-error.tsx` (must render its own `<html>`/`<body>`).

---

### 🟠 MEDIUM — Missing Security Headers in Middleware
**File:** `middleware.ts`
Headers set: `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy`, `X-DNS-Prefetch-Control`. **Missing notable headers:**
- **`Content-Security-Policy`** (CSP) — most important omission.
- **`Strict-Transport-Security`** (HSTS) — should be set in production.
- `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy` / `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy` (optional hardening).
**Fix:** Add at least a CSP and HSTS (production-only).

---

### 🟡 MEDIUM — Two Parallel Auth Systems, Middleware Only Checks One
- `src/lib/auth/session.ts` + `middleware.ts` use a **custom JWT cookie** (`pph-session`, HS256 via `jose`).
- `src/lib/supabase/server.ts` + `client.ts` use **`@supabase/ssr`** cookie-based sessions (anon key + RLS).
The middleware's `verifySession` only inspects the custom `pph-session` JWT — it never touches Supabase auth cookies. The Supabase server client's `setAll` swallows errors on the assumption "session refresh is handled by middleware" (per its own comment), but the middleware here does **not** refresh Supabase sessions. If Supabase Auth is actually used for user identity, sessions will silently fail to refresh and eventually 401. If Supabase is data-only (anon + RLS) this is fine — but the coexistence is confusing and under-documented. **Recommend clarifying which system is the source of truth.**

---

### 🟡 LOW/MEDIUM — Middleware Verifies JWT on Every Request (incl. public)
**File:** `middleware.ts` — `verifySession(request)` runs unconditionally for every matched route (home page, public gallery, API routes, etc.), performing an async `jwtVerify` even when the route is neither protected nor an auth route. This adds avoidable latency to all public traffic.
**Fix:** Only call `verifySession` when `isProtectedRoute || isAuthRoute`.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Middleware Is Authn-Only (No Authz)
`PROTECTED_PREFIXES = ["/dashboard"]` and `AUTH_ROUTES = ["/login"]` only check authentication, not authorization. Any authenticated user (any role) passes the middleware to every `/dashboard/*` sub-route. Role enforcement happens only inside server components/actions via `requireRole`/`requireOwner`/etc. (`src/lib/auth/permissions.ts`). This is an acceptable pattern, but means a CLIENT could navigate to e.g. `/dashboard/settings` and only get blocked server-side after render/attempted action. **Worth documenting** as a deliberate layered-authz decision.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Non-null Assertions on Supabase Env Vars
**Files:** `src/lib/supabase/server.ts`, `client.ts`, `admin.ts` — all use `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!`, `...ANON_KEY!`, `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!`. If an env var is missing, `createClient` receives `undefined` and fails at request time with a cryptic error rather than failing fast at startup. The admin client (`admin.ts`) is especially sensitive since it bypasses RLS via the service role key.
**Fix:** Validate env presence explicitly and throw a clear error.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Cookie Options / Minor
`src/lib/auth/config.ts` — `cookieOptions` includes a redundant `name` field (the cookie name is already `SESSION_COOKIE_NAME` passed to `store.set()` in `createSession`). Harmless but redundant. `sameSite: "lax"`, `secure: production-only`, `httpOnly: true` are all correct.

---

### ✅ Things Done Correctly
- **Provider order** in `src/app/layout.tsx` is correct: `ThemeProvider` (outermost) → `SessionProvider` → `QueryProvider` → children + `<Toaster>`. Theme wraps everything (needed for `attribute="class"` SSR), and Session sits outside Query so queries could access session context if wired up.
- **`suppressHydrationWarning`** is on `<html>` (correct for `next-themes`), `dir="rtl"`/`lang="ar"` set.
- **`QueryProvider`** uses `useState(() => new QueryClient(...))` — correct pattern to avoid recreating the client per render; sensible defaults.
- **`ThemeProvider`** is a thin client wrapper around `next-themes` — correctly marked `"use client"`.
- **JWT/session core** (`session.ts`): `requireUser` correctly distinguishes `JWTExpired` → `ExpiredSessionError` vs other failures → `UnauthorizedError`; `requireRole` throws `ForbiddenError`; `destroySession` deletes cookie. Secret read via `TextEncoder` works in Edge runtime. Good.
- **Error classes** (`errors.ts`) are well-structured with codes.
- **Middleware redirect to login** passes `pathname` (always internal) — safe at that point; the vulnerability is only on the receiving login page.
- **`createSupabaseAdminClient`** correctly disables `autoRefreshToken`/`persistSession` and is server-only.
- **`error.tsx`** is a proper client component with `reset()`.

### Remaining Gaps
- I did not inspect `src/actions/auth/login.ts` (the `loginAction` invoked by the login page) to see whether it validates the redirect target server-side; the open-redirect risk exists regardless because the **client** performs `router.push(redirect)` before/after the action. Recommend auditing that action next.
- I did not verify whether any dashboard page reads `useSession()` (the dead-context impact depends on usage); a grep for `useSession` across `src/` would quantify blast radius.
args.next_steps:
1. Fix the open redirect in `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx` — validate the `redirect` param is a single leading-slash relative path before `router.push`.
2. Wire the `SessionProvider` to the server session (seed from a server component) or remove it if unused; set initial `status` to `"loading"` and hydrate.
3. Add `src/app/global-error.tsx` to catch root-layout/provider errors.
4. Add `Content-Security-Policy` and `Strict-Transport-Security` headers in `middleware.ts`.
5. Only run `verifySession` for protected/auth routes to cut public-route latency.
6. Add explicit env-var validation in the three Supabase client factories.
7. Audit `src/actions/auth/login.ts` for any additional redirect handling, and grep `useSession` usage to confirm dead-context impact.

An attacker can craft `https://yoursite.com/login?redirect=https://evil.com` (or `//evil.com`, `/\evil.com`) and, after a victim logs in, redirect them off-site. This is a textbook open redirect, often leveraged for phishing. The middleware sets the param safely (`loginUrl.searchParams.set("redirect", pathname)` — always internal), but the login page trusts arbitrary client-supplied values.
**Fix:** Validate that `redirect` starts with `/` and does not start with `//` / `/\`, else fall back to `ROUTES.DASHBOARD`.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL — SessionProvider Never Synced With Server Session (Dead Context)
**File:** `src/providers/session-provider.tsx`
The provider initializes `user=null` / `status="unauthenticated"` and exposes `setUser`/`setStatus`, but **nothing ever populates it from the server**. I confirmed `src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx` contains no `useSession`/`setUser`/`getSession`/`getCurrentUser` calls. Consequences:
- Any component calling `useSession()` always sees an unauthenticated user on the client.
- The `status` type includes `"loading"` but it is **never used** — initial state jumps straight to `"unauthenticated"`, so there is no loading/hydration phase.
- This is an SSR/client boundary break: the server knows the session (via `getSession()`/JWT), but the client context is permanently stale.
**Fix:** Either hydrate the provider from a server component (pass the server `SessionUser` as a prop and seed initial state), or fetch session on mount and set status to `"loading"` until resolved. Better still, consider using Next.js `cookies()` server-side and dropping the client session context if not needed.

---

### 🟠 HIGH — No `global-error.tsx` (Root Layout Errors Unhandled)
Confirmed: `src/app/global-error.tsx` does **not** exist. Only `src/app/error.tsx` (and `src/app/p/[token]/error.tsx`) exist. By Next.js design, `error.tsx` does **not** catch errors thrown in the root `layout.tsx` itself. If a provider (`ThemeProvider`/`SessionProvider`/`QueryProvider`) or the `<Toaster>` throws during root layout render, the user gets the default Next.js error page, not the branded `error.tsx`. 
**Fix:** Add `src/app/global-error.tsx` (must render its own `<html>`/`<body>`).

---

### 🟠 MEDIUM — Missing Security Headers in Middleware
**File:** `middleware.ts`
Headers set: `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy`, `X-DNS-Prefetch-Control`. **Missing notable headers:**
- **`Content-Security-Policy`** (CSP) — most important omission.
- **`Strict-Transport-Security`** (HSTS) — should be set in production.
- `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy` / `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy` (optional hardening).
**Fix:** Add at least a CSP and HSTS (production-only).

---

### 🟡 MEDIUM — Two Parallel Auth Systems, Middleware Only Checks One
- `src/lib/auth/session.ts` + `middleware.ts` use a **custom JWT cookie** (`pph-session`, HS256 via `jose`).
- `src/lib/supabase/server.ts` + `client.ts` use **`@supabase/ssr`** cookie-based sessions (anon key + RLS).
The middleware's `verifySession` only inspects the custom `pph-session` JWT — it never touches Supabase auth cookies. The Supabase server client's `setAll` swallows errors on the assumption "session refresh is handled by middleware" (per its own comment), but the middleware here does **not** refresh Supabase sessions. If Supabase Auth is actually used for user identity, sessions will silently fail to refresh and eventually 401. If Supabase is data-only (anon + RLS) this is fine — but the coexistence is confusing and under-documented. **Recommend clarifying which system is the source of truth.**

---

### 🟡 LOW/MEDIUM — Middleware Verifies JWT on Every Request (incl. public)
**File:** `middleware.ts` — `verifySession(request)` runs unconditionally for every matched route (home page, public gallery, API routes, etc.), performing an async `jwtVerify` even when the route is neither protected nor an auth route. This adds avoidable latency to all public traffic.
**Fix:** Only call `verifySession` when `isProtectedRoute || isAuthRoute`.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Middleware Is Authn-Only (No Authz)
`PROTECTED_PREFIXES = ["/dashboard"]` and `AUTH_ROUTES = ["/login"]` only check authentication, not authorization. Any authenticated user (any role) passes the middleware to every `/dashboard/*` sub-route. Role enforcement happens only inside server components/actions via `requireRole`/`requireOwner`/etc. (`src/lib/auth/permissions.ts`). This is an acceptable pattern, but means a CLIENT could navigate to e.g. `/dashboard/settings` and only get blocked server-side after render/attempted action. **Worth documenting** as a deliberate layered-authz decision.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Non-null Assertions on Supabase Env Vars
**Files:** `src/lib/supabase/server.ts`, `client.ts`, `admin.ts` — all use `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!`, `...ANON_KEY!`, `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!`. If an env var is missing, `createClient` receives `undefined` and fails at request time with a cryptic error rather than failing fast at startup. The admin client (`admin.ts`) is especially sensitive since it bypasses RLS via the service role key.
**Fix:** Validate env presence explicitly and throw a clear error.

---

### 🟡 LOW — Cookie Options / Minor
`src/lib/auth/config.ts` — `cookieOptions` includes a redundant `name` field (the cookie name is already `SESSION_COOKIE_NAME` passed to `store.set()` in `createSession`). Harmless but redundant. `sameSite: "lax"`, `secure: production-only`, `httpOnly: true` are all correct.

---

### ✅ Things Done Correctly
- **Provider order** in `src/app/layout.tsx` is correct: `ThemeProvider` (outermost) → `SessionProvider` → `QueryProvider` → children + `<Toaster>`. Theme wraps everything (needed for `attribute="class"` SSR), and Session sits outside Query so queries could access session context if wired up.
- **`suppressHydrationWarning`** is on `<html>` (correct for `next-themes`), `dir="rtl"`/`lang="ar"` set.
- **`QueryProvider`** uses `useState(() => new QueryClient(...))` — correct pattern to avoid recreating the client per render; sensible defaults.
- **`ThemeProvider`** is a thin client wrapper around `next-themes` — correctly marked `"use client"`.
- **JWT/session core** (`session.ts`): `requireUser` correctly distinguishes `JWTExpired` → `ExpiredSessionError` vs other failures → `UnauthorizedError`; `requireRole` throws `ForbiddenError`; `destroySession` deletes cookie. Secret read via `TextEncoder` works in Edge runtime. Good.
- **Error classes** (`errors.ts`) are well-structured with codes.
- **Middleware redirect to login** passes `pathname` (always internal) — safe at that point; the vulnerability is only on the receiving login page.
- **`createSupabaseAdminClient`** correctly disables `autoRefreshToken`/`persistSession` and is server-only.
- **`error.tsx`** is a proper client component with `reset()`.

### Remaining Gaps
- I did not inspect `src/actions/auth/login.ts` (the `loginAction` invoked by the login page) to see whether it validates the redirect target server-side; the open-redirect risk exists regardless because the **client** performs `router.push(redirect)` before/after the action. Recommend auditing that action next.
- I did not verify whether any dashboard page reads `useSession()` (the dead-context impact depends on usage); a grep for `useSession` across `src/` would quantify blast radius.

Next steps:
1. Fix the open redirect in `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx` — validate the `redirect` param is a single leading-slash relative path before `router.push`.
2. Wire the `SessionProvider` to the server session (seed from a server component) or remove it if unused; set initial `status` to `"loading"` and hydrate.
3. Add `src/app/global-error.tsx` to catch root-layout/provider errors.
4. Add `Content-Security-Policy` and `Strict-Transport-Security` headers in `middleware.ts`.
5. Only run `verifySession` for protected/auth routes to cut public-route latency.
6. Add explicit env-var validation in the three Supabase client factories.
7. Audit `src/actions/auth/login.ts` for any additional redirect handling, and grep `useSession` usage to confirm dead-context impact.

functionDeclarations.name: glob
functionDeclarations.description: Efficiently finds files matching specific glob patterns (e.g., `src/**/*.ts`, `**/*.md`), returning absolute paths sorted by modification time (newest first). Ideal for quickly locating files based on their name or path structure, especially in large codebases.

pattern.description: The glob pattern to match against (e.g., '**/*.py', 'docs/*.md').

path.description: Optional: The absolute path to the directory to search within. If omitted, searches the root directory.

case_sensitive.description: Optional: Whether the search should be case-sensitive. Defaults to false.

include_hidden.description: Optional: Whether to descend into dot-prefixed directories (e.g. '.git/', '.codely-cli/') and match dot-prefixed files. Defaults to false to mirror search content tools. Set to true to scan hidden paths explicitly.

maxResults.description: Optional: Maximum number of file paths to return (default: 100). Use to limit output for broad patterns. When the cap is hit the result is paginated; pass 'offset' set to the previous offset+maxResults to continue.

offset.description: Optional: Number of matching file paths to skip before returning results. Use this to continue after a truncated result's next offset.

functionDeclarations.name: list_directory
functionDeclarations.description: Lists the names of files and subdirectories directly within a specified directory path. Can optionally ignore entries matching provided glob patterns.

path.description: The absolute path to the directory to list (must be absolute, not relative)

ignore.description: List of glob patterns to ignore

functionDeclarations.name: read_file
functionDeclarations.description: Reads and returns the content of a specified file. By default reads up to 2000 lines starting from the beginning of the file. Files whose selected range exceeds 256 KB are truncated with an advisory; use 'offset' and 'limit' only if the file is too large to read at once — it's recommended to read the whole file by not providing these parameters. Handles text files. For images, PDFs, videos, audio or other multimedia files, this tool cannot present their contents to the model — use the 'analyze_multimedia' tool instead.

absolute_path.description: The absolute path to the file to read (e.g., '/home/user/project/file.txt'). Relative paths are not supported. You must provide an absolute path.

offset.description: Optional: For text files, the 0-based line number to start reading from. Only provide if the file is too large to read at once. Note: search_file_content reports matches as 1-based `Lxx` line numbers; to jump from a hit at `L42` here, subtract 1 — pass `offset: 41` (i.e. `offset = lineNumber - 1`).

limit.description: Optional: For text files, the maximum number of lines to read. Only provide if the file is too large to read at once.

functionDeclarations.name: run_shell_command
functionDeclarations.description:
This tool executes a given shell command using the best available Windows shell (PowerShell Core, Windows PowerShell, or Command Prompt).

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

command.description: Exact command to execute using the best available Windows shell (automatically selected from PowerShell Core, Windows PowerShell, or Command Prompt). Quote any file paths containing spaces with double quotes.

description.description: Brief description of the command for the user. Be specific and concise. Ideally a single sentence. Can be up to 3 sentences for clarity. No line breaks.

directory.description: (OPTIONAL) The absolute path of the directory to run the command in. If not provided, the project root directory is used. Must be a directory within the workspace and must already exist.

functionDeclarations.name: search_file_content
functionDeclarations.description: Searches for a regular expression pattern within the content of files in a specified directory, a specific file, or the current working directory. The search backend is ripgrep, so regex patterns use ripgrep/Rust regex syntax, not JavaScript RegExp syntax. If you need to search literal text that contains regex metacharacters (for example $, {, [, (, or \), set fixed_strings: true or -F: true instead of escaping by hand. Can filter files by a glob pattern when searching directories. By default, returns matching lines with file paths and line numbers; set output_mode to "files_with_matches" for just file paths or "count" for per-file match totals. Each matched line is truncated at 500 characters with a "[line truncated from N chars]" suffix to keep minified or base64 content from blowing up context.

pattern.description: The regular expression pattern to search for within file contents. This is interpreted by ripgrep/Rust regex, not JavaScript RegExp; avoid JS-only features such as lookbehind and backreferences. Regex syntax errors are returned by ripgrep. For literal text, especially strings containing regex metacharacters like $, {, [, (, or \, prefer fixed_strings: true or -F: true. When passing JSON args, escape only for JSON once: to match literal ${, use pattern "\\$\\{" (the regex engine sees \$\{), not "\\\\$\\\\{".

path.description: Optional: A path relative to the workspace root, or an absolute path to a directory or file to search within. If a file path is provided, searches only within that file. If a directory path is provided, searches all files in that directory. If omitted, searches the current working directory.

glob.description: Optional: A glob pattern to filter which files are searched (e.g., '*.js', '*.{ts,tsx}', 'src/**'). Single string only — combine multiple patterns via whitespace/comma separation (e.g. '*.js *.ts', '*.js,*.ts') or brace expansion ('*.{ts,tsx}'). Only applicable when searching directories, ignored for file searches. If omitted, searches all files (respecting potential global ignores).

output_mode.description: Optional: Output format. 'content' (default) returns matching lines with line numbers and optional context; 'files_with_matches' returns only the list of matching file paths; 'count' returns per-file match counts.

maxResults.description: Optional: Maximum number of matches to return to prevent context overflow (default: 250, max: 1000). Values above 1000 are clamped automatically. Use lower values for broad searches, higher for specific searches. Pass 0 for unlimited.

head_limit.description: Optional: Claude Code aligned alias for 'maxResults'. When both are provided, head_limit wins. Pass 0 for unlimited (use sparingly — large result sets waste context).

offset.description: Optional: Number of matches to skip before returning results. Use this to continue after a truncated result's next offset.

-A.description: Optional: Number of context lines to show after each match. Only applies to 'content' output_mode. Alias for ripgrep's -A.

-B.description: Optional: Number of context lines to show before each match. Only applies to 'content' output_mode. Alias for ripgrep's -B.

-C.description: Optional: Number of context lines to show before and after each match. Only applies to 'content' output_mode. Alias for ripgrep's -C.

context.description: Optional: Number of context lines to show before and after each match. Only applies to 'content' output_mode. Alias for -C.

-n.description: Optional: Whether to include line numbers in 'content' output. Defaults to true; set to false to omit them.

-i.description: Optional: Case-insensitive matching. Defaults to false (case-sensitive), matching ripgrep behavior.

fixed_strings.description: Optional: Treat the pattern as a literal string instead of a regular expression. Alias for -F.

-F.description: Optional: Treat the pattern as a literal string instead of a regular expression. Alias for fixed_strings.

type.description: Optional: ripgrep file type filter (rg --type). Common types: js, py, rust, go, java, etc. Accepts any alias from `rg --type-list`; the value is forwarded verbatim to ripgrep.

multiline.description: Optional: Enable multiline matching where patterns may span newlines (passes -U and --multiline-dotall to ripgrep). Only meaningful for 'content' output_mode. ^/$ anchors keep line boundaries behavior for portability across search backends.

include_hidden.description: Optional: Whether to descend into dot-prefixed directories (e.g. '.git/', '.codely-cli/') and match dot-prefixed files. Defaults to false to mirror ripgrep's default hidden-skip behaviour. Set to true to scan hidden paths explicitly (passes --hidden to ripgrep).
